Title: First Korean Objects Enter The Met Collection
Description: The Met received its first Korean objects through the Crosby Brown Collection. The donation included eight Korean musical instruments, marking the beginning of the museum's Korean collection.
Media Caption: Haegŭm (해금), 19th century Korean bowed string instrument from the Crosby Brown Collection, acquired in 1889

Title: Early Korean Ceramics Acquired
Description: The Met acquired a fifteenth-century Buncheong ware bowl known as "Gyeongju Jangheunggo," one of the museum's earliest Korean ceramic acquisitions.
Media Caption: Buncheong ware dish with chrysanthemum motifs and the inscription "Gyeongju Jangheunggo," acquired by The Met in 1893

Title: Opening of the Arts of Korea Gallery
Description: The permanent Arts of Korea Gallery officially opened in June 1998 with support from the Korea Foundation and the Samsung Foundation of Culture.
Media Caption: Cover of Arts of Korea, the publication accompanying the opening of The Met's permanent Arts of Korea Gallery in 1998

Title: Art of the Korean Renaissance, 1400–1600
Description: This international loan exhibition presented works from the early Joseon dynasty, including painting, ceramics, sculpture, lacquerware, and metalwork.
Media Caption: Cover of Art of the Korean Renaissance, 1400–1600, published alongside the 2009 exhibition at The Met

Title: Silla: Korea's Golden Kingdom
Description: This exhibition focused on the art and culture of the Silla kingdom and featured royal gold regalia, Buddhist art, and objects related to Silk Road exchange.
Media Caption: A bronze bodhisattva from the seventh century displayed in Silla: Korea's Golden Kingdom at The Metropolitan Museum of Art

Title: Korea: 100 Years of Collecting at The Met
Description: This exhibition reflected on one hundred years of collecting Korean art at The Met and examined how the museum's Korean collection expanded over time.
Media Caption: Seated bodhisattva (left attendant of a triad), featured in Korea: 100 Years of Collecting at The Met

Title: Diamond Mountains: Travel and Nostalgia in Korean Art
Description: This exhibition explored the cultural meaning of Korea's Diamond Mountains through paintings, photographs, ceramics, and contemporary artworks.
column9: Video introduction to Diamond Mountains: Travel and Nostalgia in Korean Art at The Metropolitan Museum of Art

Title: Shell and Resin: Korean Mother-of-Pearl and Lacquer
Description: This exhibition examined the history and craftsmanship of Korean mother-of-pearl lacquerware from the Joseon dynasty to the modern period.
Media Caption: Trefoil-shaped covered box with chrysanthemum decoration, featured in Shell and Resin: Korean Mother-of-Pearl and Lacquer

Title: Jegi: Korean Ritual Objects
Description: This exhibition introduced Korean ritual vessels known as jegi and explored their historical and ceremonial significance.
Media Caption: Installation view of the exhibition Jegi: Korean Ritual Objects

Title: Lineages: Korean Art at The Met
Description: This exhibition commemorated the twenty-fifth anniversary of The Met's Korean Gallery and presented works from the twelfth century to contemporary Korean art through themes such as lines, people, places, and objects.
Media Caption: Exhibition poster for Lineages: Korean Art at The Met

Title: Colorful Korea: The Lea R. Sneider Collection
Description: This exhibition presents approximately one hundred works from the fifth century to the present from the Lea R. Sneider Collection, highlighting the colorful material culture and everyday aesthetics of Korean art.
Media Caption: Lea R. Sneider standing in front of a Jogakbo (patchwork wrapping cloth), reflecting her interest in Korean art and material culture
